feedback.positive: Excellent explanation. You clearly understand the divide & conquer approach and how binary search halves the search space each iteration.
feedback.improvement: Make sure to mention that the array MUST be sorted, explain how the search space is halved, and state the O(log n) time complexity.

feedback.positive: Great job. You explained the basic swapping mechanism and the bubble-up behavior well.
feedback.improvement: Don't forget to mention that the largest element 'bubbles' to the end in each pass. State the O(n²) worst-case complexity.

feedback.positive: You've clearly articulated the divide-and-conquer strategy and merge step. Good understanding of O(n log n) complexity.
feedback.improvement: Make sure to explain that the array is divided in half recursively, and that the merge step combines two sorted halves. Mention O(n) space complexity.

feedback.positive: Excellent breakdown of the QuickSort algorithm. Your pivot selection and partitioning explanation was clear.
feedback.improvement: Mention how pivot choice affects performance. Explain that worst case O(n²) occurs with already sorted arrays and bad pivot selection.

feedback.positive: You demonstrated a solid understanding of BFS and its level-order exploration using a queue.
feedback.improvement: Remember to mention that BFS uses a Queue (FIFO), explores level by level, and can find shortest paths in unweighted graphs. Time: O(V+E).

feedback.positive: Great explanation of DFS! You correctly identified the backtracking nature and stack/recursion usage.
feedback.improvement: Mention that DFS uses a Stack (or recursion), explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Useful for cycle detection and topological sorting.

feedback.positive: Well explained! You covered the hash function, key-value mapping, and collision handling clearly.
feedback.improvement: Explain how the hash function maps keys to indices, mention O(1) average lookup, and describe collision resolution (chaining vs open addressing).

feedback.positive: You clearly explained the two-pointer technique and when to apply it. Good understanding of its O(n) efficiency.
feedback.improvement: Make sure to mention that two pointers works best on sorted arrays, with pointers moving from opposite ends toward each other based on comparison with target.
